coredata.dc:title: Strong reverse saturable absorption and negative nonlinear refractive index in S and N co-doped GQDs at 532 nm CW laser
coredata.dc:creator: Kumara, K.
Shetty, Thangalapalu Chandra Shekhara
Patil, Parutagouda Shankaragouda
Dharmaprakash, Sampyady Medappa
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a bottom up pyrolysis approach is pursued in the preparation of sulphur: nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs). For the first time, 5V direct current light emitting diode (LED) is used as an excitation source and the influence of solvent’s nature such as dipole moment and polarity on fluorescence is studied. The investigated third order nonlinear optical behaviour reveals a strong reverse saturable absorption (RSA) and negative nonlinear refractive (NLR) index at 532 nm wavelength of continuous wave (CW) laser. The thermal nonlinearity in closed aperture is addressed employing thermal lens model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
LED
Luminescence
Optical materials and properties
NLO

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra (a), FL spectra (b), and FL spectra of CTU2 (c) & (d).
Z-scan OA (a) and CA (b), thermal lens fitting for GQDs (c) and CTU2 (d).
TNLO parameters of GQDs and SN-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Strong reverse saturable absorption and negative nonlinear refractive index in S and N co-doped GQDs at 532 nm CW laser

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Mangalore University, Mangalore 574 199, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Post Graduation Studies and Research in Physics, St Aloysius College (Autonomous), Mangalore 574 003, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Post Graduation Studies and Research in Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, K.L.E. Institute of Technology, Opposite Airport, Gokul, Hubballi 578 030, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a bottom up pyrolysis approach is pursued in the preparation of sulphur: nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs). For the first time, 5V direct current light emitting diode (LED) is used as an excitation source and the influence of solvent’s nature such as dipole moment and polarity on fluorescence is studied. The investigated third order nonlinear optical behaviour reveals a strong reverse saturable absorption (RSA) and negative nonlinear refractive (NLR) index at 532 nm wavelength of continuous wave (CW) laser. The thermal nonlinearity in closed aperture is addressed employing thermal lens model.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, graphene derivatives pioneered photonic and optoelectronic research owing to their exceptional optical and electrical properties [1]. The chemically active functional groups and optical properties of few graphene derivatives such as graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are employed in biosensor, drug delivery, bio-imaging applications etc [2–4]. The GQDs is a multifunctional material which contains oxygen enriched functional moieties of graphene sheets. Unlike graphene, the bandgap and quantum confinement effect exhibited by GQDs are responsible for strong fluorescence (FL) emission which is independent of excitation wavelength [4–7]. This FL emission is mainly emanated from the uniform size distribution of conjugate sp2 domains, defect sites, and the edge functional groups.

In general, this type of pi-conjugate structure in the materials exhibits an immense NLO activity. Here, the NLO behaviour of the graphene derivatives is prominent due their large extension of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. The saturable absorption (SA), reverse saturable absorption (RSA) and nonlinear refraction (NLR) under different laser regimes and intensities are observed so far. But, the NLO properties of GQDs are not explored till date despite of enormous sp2 hybridization present in it. However, the changes in NLR properties from negative to positive is observed in biofilm of human ovarian cancers cells when it is treated with GQDs under He-Ne continuous wave (CW) laser [8] and the optical switching behaviour (changing from SA to RSA) of GO quantum dots under nanosecond laser at 532 nm is reported [7]. In addition, Sulphur (S) and Nitrogen (N) atoms doped into GQDs can enhance the optical activities by contributing delocalized electrons, it activates more optical sites and defect density for better FL. Delocalized electrons in π-conjugation can further improve the NLO properties. In the present work, the NLO properties of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) is investigated using diode pumped solid state (DPSS) CW laser of wavelength 532 nm.

GQD and SN-GQDs were prepared from the pyrolysis of citric acid [9] and thiourea with different molar ratios. Firstly, 2:0, 2:1, 2:2, and 2:3 mM ratios of citric acid and thiourea were taken in different 50 mL beakers and labeled as GQD, CTU1, CTU2 and CTU3 respectively. The beakers were placed in a hot air oven maintained at 453 K for 1 h. Finally, a black solid product was obtained which was used in further analysis.

Powder X-ray diffractometer (PXRD, Rigaku Miniflex 600) λ = 1.540 Å with Si-filtered CuKα1 radiation, STR 500 Confocal Raman spectrometer (DPSS laser, λ = 532 nm, Power = 5 W), Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-1800), FL was recorded using Ocean Optics USB4000 Spectra suit (DC 5V LED, λex = 375 nm and 452 nm). Diode Pumped Solid State (DPSS) CW laser beam operating at λ = 532 nm (Max. input Power = 200 mW).

The PXRD peaks (Fig. 1 a) at 23.6°, 26°, 23.9° and 24.7° obtained for GQDs, CTU1, CTU2 and CTU3 can be attributed to the (0 0 2) crystalline plane confirms the GQDs formation [4,9,10]. The respective calculated interlayer spacing is found to be 0.4 nm, 0.46 nm, 0.51 nm and 0.5 nm. From Raman spectra, the ordered (sp2) and disordered structure (sp3) vibration peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1375 cm−1 represent G and D band, respectively (Fig. 1b) endorse the GQD formation. The intercalation of ‘S’ atoms into the conjugated carbon led to higher ID/IG ratio in case of CTU2 compared to other samples which can be attributed to an inflated defect density of sp2 domains in-plane graphitic crystallite [11]. However, the incomplete carbonization of citric acid in GQD results in the broadening of the D band and higher ID /IG ratio implies more disorderness. The perfect domains of graphene present in the GQD and SN-GQDs is calculated using the equation,
L a
n m
= 560 /
E
λ
4
.
(
I D
/
I G
)
- 1
, where ‘E λ ’ is the excitation energy (eV) and La is in-plane crystallite size (nm) [12]. GQDs of La ∼ 84 nm upon functionalization reduced to 31 nm, 20 nm and 40 nm for CTU1, CTU2 and CTU3, respectively. So, these results suggest declining in crystalline size and increase of the amorphous carbon upon reduction [13–15] on using thiourea.

The detailed analysis of the FTIR spectra, elemental analysis using EDS and surface morphology of GQD and SN-GQDs are discussed in detail in the Supplementary Information Part 1, 2 and 3, respectively.

As shown in the Fig. 2 a, the UV–Vis absorbance peaks centered at 345 nm and 270 nm are attributed to n → π * transition in the CO and π → π * transitions in sp2 carbon (CC, CN) respectively [16] and the inset Fig. 2a shows the absorption peak of GQDs [9].

Fig. 2b presents the FL spectra of the SN-GQDs in Dimethylformamide (DMF) and inset depicts the FL spectra for GQDs. An increase in the photoluminescence (PL) intensity is observed in N-doped GQDs due to availability of delocalized electrons to the π system from pyridinic-N structures [3,17] and the formation of additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon in ‘S’ doped GQDs are responsible for intense PL emission [11,18]. In the present study, the addition of energy levels, the nonbonding electrons in the π-conjugate system and optical sites (disorder) in SN-GQDSs accounts for strong FL emission. The FL of CAU2 exhibits strong emission due to increase in defect density sp2 domains in-plane graphitic crystallite. The Supplementary Information Part 4 elucidates in detail the calculation of relative quantum yields (rQY) and solvent’s environment on FL intensity of SN-GQD.

The investigated third order NLO (TNLO) properties of GQDs and SN-GQDs exhibit RSA type NLA for open aperture (OA) and peak-valley configuration for closed aperture (CA) Z-scan curves as shown in the Fig. 3 . The π-conjugation length and the functional groups notably impact on the NLO parameters [13]. The NLO absorption (β) values are found to vary with an increase in thiourea concentration (Table 1 ) and this can be explained using ID/IG intensity ratio and the functional groups. In SN-GQDs, the addition of thiourea can trim some of the functional groups and improve π-conjugation length (ID/IG < 1). So that, ‘β’ values increase with increase in thiourea for CTU1, CTU2, and CTU3. The CTU2 shows large ‘β’ value due to having large maximum extent π-conjugation length and delocalized electrons. In general, an intra molecular charge transfer between donor and acceptor leads to high charge transfer in the molecules [19]. The presence ‘N’ and ‘S’ in GQDs may be facilitated to increase in the high charge transfer to achieve large ‘β’ value. However, the disruptions of pi networks (ID/IG = 0.62) is due to surpass of thiourea over citric acid in CTU3 resulting in the declining of ‘β’ value.

The negative nonlinear refractive index is assigned to the self-defocusing effect of undoped and doped GQDs obtained from the CA curves. This self-defocusing effect arises mainly due to the linear absorption of solvents which occurs on interaction with CW laser. It led to a nonlocal heating effect [20,21]. The asymmetry curve about the principle focal point in CA suggests thermal nonlinearity dominating in the CW mode. Therefore, thermal lens model has been used to fit the experimental CA curve shown in Fig. 3c and d. The Z-scan theory and thermal lens model [20,22] have been explained in the Supplementary Information Part 5.

The bottom up pyrolysis method has been employed in the preparation of cost effective GQDs and SN-GQDs. The defect density and sp2 domains improves FL and NLO activities, so that, β increases up to 7.6 × 10–6 cm W−1 from 0.14 × 10–6 cm W−1. These materials exhibit RSA type NLA behaviour. The thermal nonlinearity is dominated due to interaction of solvent and CW laser. The materials with these properties can decisive in opto-electronics and NLO based devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: K. Kumara, thankful to Mangalore University for providing ST fellwoship (MU/SCT RF/CR17/2013-2014) to carry out the research and P.S. Patil is thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India, Young Scientist Research Grant (SB/FTP/PS-021/2014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2018.09.120.

ce:section.ce:para:
